data.title: Voya Corporate Leaders Trust Fund B Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

A passively managed grantor trust that invests in most of the same 30 blue-chip companies bought in 1935 or their direct descendants.

Market review

U.S. stocks continued their upward trajectory during the fourth quarter following Donald Trump's presidential victory, with the S&P 500 Index (SPX)(SP500) rising by 2.41% and the Nasdaq Composite advancing by 6.17%. The consumer discretionary and communication services sectors led, while materials and healthcare lagged. Large cap stocks outperformed small caps, and growth stocks significantly beat value stocks.

The U.S. bond market struggled during the quarter on concerns about sticky inflation and the U.S. Federal Reserve's more conservative rate-cut path. The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index declined by 3.06% and the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield rose by more than 80 basis points (bp), ending the quarter at 4.58%. The Fed cut rates by 25 bp in November and December. However, the central bank now projects just two rate cuts in 2025, reflecting a more cautious stance in response to strong economic data.

An investor should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of the Fund(s) carefully before investing. For a free copy of the Fund's prospectus or summary prospectus, which contains this and other information, visit us at www.voyainvestments.com or call (800) 992-0180. Please read all materials carefully before investing.

Portfolio review

The underperformance during the quarter was driven primarily by selection effects. Stock selection effects within energy, industrials and financials sectors were the main detractors for performance. Relative overweights to industrials and energy were also major detractors. Key individual detractors included overweights in Union Pacific Corp. (UNP), Marathon Petroleum Corp. (MPC) and Linde PLC (LIN).

Stock selection effects in the utilities sector was a contributor. Not allocating to the health care sector contributed. Key individual contributors for the period included, not owning Microsoft Corp. (MSFT), Eli Lilly and Co. (LLY) and UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH). As of the end of the reporting period the Strategy's largest sector overweights included the industrials and energy sectors, and to a lesser extent materials. The largest underweight sectors were information technology, consumer discretionary and health care. The Trust does not currently hold positions within the information technology or real estate sectors. Sector exposures are purely a function of the strategy's quantitative investment discipline, however, and are not actively managed.

Outlook

After months of noise surrounding the U.S. presidential election, markets have now refocused on macroeconomic data, which offer mixed signals. Key concerns include global geopolitical tensions, especially around tariffs, and deteriorating sentiment tied to mega-cap positioning and broader market weakness. Despite these challenges, U.S. equities should continue to benefit from robust consumer spending, optimism around artificial intelligence and solid corporate earnings. U.S. inflation is projected at 2.5% for 2025, but the new administration's policies could reverse the disinflation trend.

Holdings Details:

Companies mentioned in this report percentage of Portfolio investments, as of 12/31/24: Union Pacific Corp. 38.50%, Marathon Petroleum Corp. 10.88%, Linde PLC. 9.38%, Microsoft Corp. 0.00%, Eli Lilly and Co. 0.00% and UnitedHealth Group Inc. 0.00%. 0% indicates that the security is no longer in the portfolio. Portfolio holdings are subject to change on a daily basis.

The Standard and Poor's 500 Index is designed to measure performance of the broad domestic economy through changes in the aggregate market value of 500 stocks representing all major industries. The index includes 500 leading companies and covers approximately 80% of available market capitalization. Index returns do not reflect fees, brokerage commissions, taxes or other expenses of investing. Investors cannot invest directly in an index.

All investing involves risks of fluctuating prices and the uncertainties of rates of return and yield inherent in investing. Investing in funds that are concentrated in a smaller number of holdings poses greater risk than investing in funds with a larger number of holdings because each investment has a greater effect on performance. The value of a participation fluctuates with the market value of the underlying portfolio securities of the Trust. The dividend income, if any, from the portfolio securities is subject to fluctuation which in turn will affect the amounts of distributions made to participants. An investor in the Trust has no assurance against loss in a declining market, and redemption at a time when the market value of the participations is less than their cost will result in a loss to the investor.

Taxation: For Federal income tax purposes, (1) the Trust will be treated as a fixed investment trust and will not be subject to Federal income tax, (2) each participant will be treated as the owner of their pro rata portion of the common stock of the corporations held by the Trust, (3) each participant will be required to include in their gross income their pro rata portion of the dividends and interest received by the Trust (including the amounts of such dividends and interest that are not distributed to participants but are used to pay the fees and expenses of the Trust), at the time such dividends and interest are received by the Trust, not at the later time such dividends and interests are distributed to participants or reinvested in additional participations, and (4) for taxable years beginning before 2026, each noncorporate (including individual) participant will generally not be permitted to deduct their pro rata portion of the fees and expenses of the Trust. Please see the prospectus for further information. The Trust is a long-term investment. Prior to investing, investors should consider their ability to pursue investment in successive trusts, if available. Investors should also be advised that the sponsor may terminate the Trust, which is a unit investment trust, earlier than the specified termination date of the Trust.

The Fund discussed may be available to you as part of your employer sponsored retirement plan. There may be additional plan level fees resulting in personal performance to vary from stated performance. Please call your benefits office for more information.

This commentary has been prepared by Voya Investment Management for informational purposes. Nothing contained herein should be construed as (i) an offer to sell or solicitation of an offer to buy any security or (ii) a recommendation as to the advisability of investing in, purchasing or selling any security. Any opinions expressed herein reflect our judgment and are subject to change. Certain of the statements contained herein are statements of future expectations and other forward-looking statements that are based on management's current views and assumptions and involve known and unknown risks and uncertainties that could cause actual results, performance or events to differ materially from those expressed or implied in such statements. Actual results, performance or events may differ materially from those in such statements due to, without limitation, (1) general economic conditions, (2) performance of financial markets, (3) interest rate levels, (4) increasing levels of loan defaults (5) changes in laws and regulations and (6) changes in the policies of governments and/or regulatory authorities.

The opinions, views and information expressed in this commentary regarding holdings are subject to change without notice. The information provided regarding holdings is not a recommendation to buy or sell any security. Portfolio holdings are fluid and are subject to daily change based on market conditions and other factors. Past Performance does not guarantee future results ©2025 Voya Investments Distributor, LLC · 230 Park Ave, New York, NY 10169 · All rights reserved.

data.title: STRF: New Perpetual Shares From Strategy (MicroStrategy), 10% Yield
data.description:
Thesis

Strategy (MSTR), formerly known as MicroStrategy, has just issued its first perpetual preferred equity, in the form of Series A 10% Preferred Equity (NASDAQ:STRF). The securities are Nasdaq listed and issued in a registered format under the Securities Act of 1933. The preferred shares offer a high 10% coupon and are cumulative.

In today's article we are going to explore this innovative new listing, benchmark its risk and rewards versus the common shares and convertible securities, and highlight the risks faced by investors in the securities.

What has been issued?

(Micro)Strategy has announced the issuance of five million shares of perpetual preferred stock:

TYSONS CORNER, Va.—(BUSINESS WIRE)—March 18, 2025—Strategy™ (Nasdaq: MSTR; STRK) today announced that, subject to market and other conditions, it intends to offer, in a public offering registered under the Securities Act of 1933, as amended (the “Securities Act”), 5,000,000 shares of Strategy’s Series A Perpetual Preferred Stock (the “perpetual strife preferred stock”).

One can find the full prospectus here, but below please find a summary of the most important analytical aspects regarding the shares:

  • Ticker: STRF (Nasdaq listing)
  • Par Value: $100/share
  • Interest Rate: 10%
  • Maturity Date: Perpetual
  • Dividend type: Cumulative
  • Payment Dates: March, June, September, December
  • Compound Dividend: Yes (10% + 100 bps)
  • Maximum compound dividend rate: 18%

The company has the option to pay a cash dividend (the dividend is only in cash), and if it skips the cash payment then the dividend accrues on the due amount, akin to a pay-in-kind instrument. The interest rates move up yearly if the cumulative interest rate is not settled:

In the event that any accumulated regular dividend on the perpetual strife preferred stock is not paid on the applicable regular dividend payment date, then additional regular dividends (“compounded dividends”) will accumulate on the amount of such unpaid regular dividend, compounded quarterly. The compounded dividend rate applicable to any unpaid regular dividend that was due on a regular dividend payment date will initially be a rate per annum equal to 10% plus 100 basis points; provided, however, that, until such regular dividend, together with compounded dividends thereon, is paid in full, such compounded dividend rate will increase by 100 basis points per annum for each subsequent regular dividend period, up to a maximum dividend rate of 18% per annum.

This feature basically represents a protection mechanism for investors who are afraid the company might choose to defer all interest payments. Via these punitive rates the prospectus makes Strategy pay higher rates if it defers interest payments.

The best way to think about STRF

STRF represents an issuance of 'classic' preferred shares. They are perpetual, and senior only to the common equity. Kindly keep in mind that MSTR has a whole slew of convertible notes outstanding, notes which are senior to the preferred shares and have in some instances mandatory convertible features:

For the purposes of investment, assume a 10-year duration for STRF while in reality do expect these preferred shares to be outstanding for the foreseeable future or until bitcoin does a parabolic up-move (think +50% or +100% from here), which would allow MSTR to issue new ones at a cheaper rate. We view STRF as a permanent form of capital.

While they have no defined maturity date, STRF is senior to the common shares and sports a large coupon, which should make this class of security much less volatile versus the common shares.

Comparison versus common shares and converts

The MSTR common shares are a leveraged play on bitcoin. As bitcoin goes up, the MSTR shares increase in value, as seen in the past few years:

Similarly, if bitcoin prices crash, the common shares will see a substantial mark-to-market loss.

The convertible securities issued by Strategy (as outlined in the table above), have a low to zero coupon, and are a play on the upside in bitcoin. The securities represent senior notes with maturity dates (i.e. they are senior unsecured debt for the company) and will have upside in case bitcoin soars and thus moves the MSTR common shares significantly up.

Conversely, the preferred shares represent a quasi-permanent form of capital, and in the case bitcoin soars (i.e. doubling from current levels), one should expect the preferred shares to stay close to par value since Strategy might be able to re-finance them cheaper. STRF is therefore a classic fixed income instrument with a perpetual maturity date (i.e. a high duration). The securities are best suited for income oriented investors who believe Strategy is here to stay (i.e. it will not go bankrupt) and that bitcoin is an asset class which holds future potential. A range bound market for bitcoin (think $50,000 to $100,000) is ideal for STRF holders since it would not introduce too much downside volatility while keeping the probability of re-finance in check.

Like any fixed income instrument, STRF will have duration risk and credit spread risk. We are going to discuss these risks further in the 'Risk Factors' section, but basically STRF will incur a forecasted -10% loss if 10-year rates move higher by 100 bps from here, and similarly a -10% loss if bitcoin crashes and the market prices the MSTR probability of default as present in credit spreads wider by 100 bps. Kindly keep in mind that STRF are the first preferred shares linked to a bitcoin company, and thus represent new pricing territory for all market participants.

Another item to note is that the issuance of innovative convertible and preferred shares is making Strategy inextricably linked with the overall bitcoin ecosystem, thus increasing its long-term survival probability.

Risk Factors and Upside

The largest risk factor here by far is the price of bitcoin. If bitcoin really crashes (think prices below $20,000), then the market will assign MSTR a higher probability of default. As per its own disclosures, MSTR's average bitcoin purchase price is roughly $66,000:

As a leveraged strategy, the more bitcoin falls below that level, the higher the probability of default. A high probability of default translates into wider credit spreads, so one can theoretically see a -30% drawdown in STRF if the market requires 14% current yields on the securities. We computed some numbers assuming a 10-year duration:

Also to note the price of bitcoin is asymmetric here. If bitcoin goes to $200,000, the preferred shares have very little upside because the market will think they will be refinanced for a cheaper rate. Unlike the converts, there is no embedded upside optionality here.

The second-largest risk factor here is represented by rates. If rates move up significantly, STRF will lose value. In our assumed 10-year duration profile, a 100 bps increase in long rates will result in a -10% drawdown.

STRF is a fixed income instrument with little upside. The securities represent a way to clip a coupon if Strategy is solvent. As discussed above, via its structure, STRF does not stand to gain if bitcoin moves up in value.

Conclusion

STRF represents exchange listed preferred shares from Strategy. The shares have been issued with a 10% cumulative coupon, and have pay in kind features to prevent the company from skipping cash payments. The securities have very little upside, and are an asymmetric play on bitcoin. While STRF is set to lose value if bitcoin crashes, it will trade close to par in an up-bitcoin scenario due to the probability of the company re-financing the shares for cheaper. The shares represent a less volatile way to speculate on Strategy's solvency and they do best in a range bound bitcoin scenario

data.title: NXP Semiconductors: Prepare For More Pain In The Coming Months
data.description:
Risk and uncertainty are beginning to play a major role in equity markets in 2025 and with that higher risk and more cyclical areas of the market are likely to suffer.

Unfortunately, there are more bad news for NXP Semiconductors N.V. (NASDAQ:NXPI) shareholders. The stock is already down by more than 18% over the past year and it appears that 2025 will be yet another challenging period.

As we could see on the graph above, the recent downward movement was not driven by broader market movements which resulted in the stock's beta falling significantly. However, that does not mean that NXPI is all of a sudden a low market risk stock, quite the contrary.

Moreover, the uptick in trading volume in recent months is another red flag that investors are losing confidence in the stock.

All that is a clear indication that investors should not rush and buy the stock, even in the face of what looks like a relatively low multiple for a business operating in the highly attractive semiconductors space.

Even though NXPI might look attractive when we take into account the stock's recent history, the rest of 2025 is likely to remain challenging, and I would abstain from imitating a position.

More Uncertainty

As a starting point, NXPI positioning within the semiconductors space puts the company at a disadvantage to some peers.

Even though long-term growth in the automotive and industrial segments is still expected to remain in double digits on an annual basis, these areas of the market are more heavily influenced by the business cycle. On the contrary, investments in Generative AI are less likely to be reduced in the face of a potential recession or a global trade war.

Within these two areas of operation, NXPI's main growth driver is expected to be the automotive sector and, more specifically - newly emerging areas of accelerated growth (shown in light blue on the graph below).

These accelerated growth areas comprise of software-defined vehicles, radar systems, electrification and vehicle connectivity. All of these are heavily influenced by demand of electric vehicles and high-end models - two areas of the automotive market that are likely to suffer the most in an event of an economic slowdown.

So far, the good news for NXPI is the company's significant exposure to the booming automotive sector in China, which could remain largely resilient on the back of increasing market share of Chinese OEMs.

The major problem of NXPI, however, stems from the fragility of the complex supply chains in the automotive sector and the potential shift towards local manufacturing on the back of the raging trade war.

The latest 25% tariff on automobile imports in the U.S. threatens to disrupt the current supply chains and could have long-term repercussions on the sector. On top of that, vehicle prices are likely to increase, which will take its toll on future demand for NXPI's chips.

As a result of all that, demand visibility is worsening, which is bad news for investors and NXPI's management when it comes to planned expansion efforts and inventory management.

Bleak Outlook For 2025

While risk and uncertainty around NXPI's business have increased significantly in recent months, the stock's lower price does not seem attractive enough to justify a buy rating.

On a historical basis, NXPI's price/sales multiple used to reflect the company's current margins until 2022. Since 2022, NXPI appears to be trading at a discount to what its current gross margins would suggest.

However, as I outlined in late 2023, these margin improvements did not seem sustainable over the long-run which was one of the main reasons why I have retained a hold rating on the stock.

Even though the latter appears as an unlikely scenario at this point in time, the record high operating margins appear unsustainable as supply chains normalize and competition continues to intensify.

Source: Seeking Alpha

Since then, things have not improved with NXPI's revenue now expected to remain flat over the coming years.

The lower revenue was blamed for NXPI's sharp fall in gross profitability during the last reported quarter which is foreshadowing what's at stake in 2025, if demand does not recover.

The uncertain demand environment is also increasing inventory risk for NXPI as its management is trying to keep the channel inventories under control. So far, some inventory digestion is anticipated in 2025, but this largely depends on-demand resiliency and the recent increase in Days inventory outstanding (DIO) and consequent worsening of the cash conversion cycle could have sustained negative implications on both margins and free cash flow.

The recently provided guidance for Q1 2025 did little to dispel any fears of lower-than-anticipated demand and with that, there's little hope that NXPI could deliver satisfactory shareholder returns in the near future.

Conclusion

As NXPI continues its descent, the stock does not appear more attractive. Macroeconomic uncertainty will most likely continue to weigh on returns in 2025 and more profitability headwinds are also to be expected. Low visibility and the disappointing Q1 2025 guidance make matters worse, and the probability of NXPI delivering satisfactory returns in the coming months is quite low.

data.title: HCA Healthcare: Attractive Even With Medicaid Risk
data.description:
Shares of HCA Healthcare (NYSE:HCA) have had a bumpy ride over the past year, while still managing a 5% gain. Republicans in Congress are ramping up their work on a budget reconciliation bill to cut taxes, and as part of this package, there may be Medicaid cuts, which could hit hospital revenue. I last covered shares in December, rating them a “buy,” and since then, they have added 16%. Given potential policy changes, now is a good time to revisit HCA. I remain bullish.

Looking first to Medicaid issues that have driven much of the headlines lately, I would note that as part of the continuing resolution passed in March, congress extended several key healthcare provisions, including providing funding for individual hospitals that provide more Medicaid care. By maintaining most healthcare-related policies that otherwise would have expired, Congress avoided making cuts to the sector, essentially allowing it to continue to run on a current policy baseline. Still, bigger changes may be coming

House Republicans are looking to potentially cut Medicaid by $880 billion over a decade, though this figure has received some pushback in the Senate. For context in fiscal 2024, $618 billion of grants were sent to states for Medicaid. To achieve this spending cut, federal Medicaid spending would need to be cut by 12-14%. Now, federal spending is only about 70% of total spending, so if states held their spending steady, total Medicaid funding would decline by about 8%.

Of course, we do not know for sure how states would react, as some could seek to increase their spending to offset federal cuts, but others could attempt to roll back eligibility alongside the Federal government. Additionally, cuts may not necessarily be proportional; hospitals could face a harsher or more lenient outcome.

Given the moderating impact of the Senate, I would expect an 8% funding cut to be the worst case scenario and would view a 5% impact as a more reasonable baseline. This cut would likely be due to a combination of lower reimbursement and tighter eligibility. As such, we could see some existing Medicaid users move onto a subsidized managed care insurance offering. I would expect this impact to be fairly modest. In aggregate, I would expect Medicaid-related revenue to have a ~3-5% headwind from legislation. This impact would likely begin to be felt in late 2025 and more fully in 2026.

This is why payer-mix is so important. HCA has a relatively modest exposure to Medicaid at about 11% of revenue. As such, the total impact to the company would likely be less than 0.5% of revenue or about $350 million, from a combination of fewer services provided and lower payments on the services it does provide. Assuming a 50% incremental margin, this is a ~$0.50 headwind to long-run EPS, or about a 2% risk to earnings. It is also worth noting that HCA already loses money on its Medicaid customers, so the ability to cut hospital payments significantly without jeopardizing rural care in particular is unclear.

There is no disputing that potential Medicaid changes are a headwind for HCA and justify a lower share price, all else equal, but the ultimate impact is likely to be relatively modest. This impact is certainly not large enough to offset all of the positives we are seeing from the company in my view.

Recapping recent results, in the company’s fourth quarter reported back on January 24th, HCA earned $6.22, which beat estimates by $0.08. Demand remains robust with 3% growth in same facility admissions. HCA also continues to recover pricing after facing a margin squeeze post-COVID. Inpatient revenue per admission rose by 4.4% to $19,800. We have also seen a favorable mix shift as inpatient surgeries rose by 2.8% while outpatient surgeries declined by 1.3%.

Thanks to these pricing gains and a normalizing labor market, margins are improving. Most importantly, salaries and benefits declined by 0.4% to 43.3% of revenue, though supply costs increased by 0.4% to 15.3%. With the labor market continuing to soften and utilization remaining strong, I expect to see some modest further decrease in salary expense as a share of revenue.

HCA remains a highly cash generative business, and it generated about $5.6 billion in free cash flow last year. It is directing all of this cash flow back to its investors. HCA primarily returns capital to investors via buybacks. Shares have a very modest 0.8% dividend yield, even after HCA increased the payout by 9% in January. In contrast, it did $6 billion of 2024 buybacks, reducing its share count by about 6%.

HCA has been able to return capital in excess of cash flow because of its strong balance sheet. It has $1.9 billion of cash and $43 billion of debt. Relative to its $13.9 billion of EBITDA, that leaves it with 3x debt/EBITDA, at the low end of its 3-4x target. Last quarter, it actually reduced its leverage target to 2.75-3.75x, which is prudent in a higher interest rate environment.

Of course, as HCA grows EBITDA, it can carry proportionately more debt while maintaining the same leverage profile. I expect it to grow EBITDA at least $800 million in 2025, providing another ~$2.5 billion of debt capacity. As such, I expect at least $8 billion of capital returns in 2025. Indeed, HCA has a new $10 billion authorization, and management aims to complete “a significant portion” in 2025. Its guided share count implies $6-8 billion of buybacks this year.

Looking at guidance more broadly. Management expects 5% revenue growth and about 7% EBITDA growth. Back in December, I was targeting at least $24-$24.50 in 2025 earnings power. Guidance points to higher EPS, partly because its share count is falling a bit more quickly than I anticipated, as buybacks may be front-half loaded.

I do not expect material intra-2025 Medicaid impacts, though with inflation slowing, I see revenue coming in at or a bit below the mid-point, leading to EPS of $24.50-$25.25.

When valuing shares of HCA, I think investors should factor in the fact that Medicaid is likely to be a headwind starting in 2026 of about $0.50, given my base case that Republicans can pass a budget bill. As such, I view run-rate earnings as closer to $24-$24.75, with HCA essentially having a $0.50 benefit from higher Medicaid this year that is unlikely to recur.

That leaves shares with a ~14x multiple and a 7% free cash flow (factoring in $150 million of Medicaid cash flow headwinds). I view this attractively, as HCA can return all free cash flow to investors and is well positioned to grow cash flow at least 3% per year, given the country’s aging population and ~2+% inflation. That means, even at $350, HCA can deliver long-term 10% returns.

It appears that shares are factoring in Medicaid headwinds, and I would remain a buyer. If Congress does not cut Medicaid, I see another $7-10 of upside. Over the next year, I see shares pushing towards $390, giving ongoing share count reduction and modest earnings growth, making HCA a buy, especially given its relatively low sensitivity to economic uncertainty.

data.title: The Trade War Trapdoor
data.description:
Stocks crashed today for no apparent reason. Try as they might, analysts couldn’t pinpoint a catalyst, then stumbled upon a trade policy proclamation from the White House yesterday. That might have been it.

Level Change 4/3/25 (%)

-4.0 Dow

-6.0 Nasdaq

-5.4 Nasdaq 100

-4.8 S&P 500

-6.7 S&P 400

-7.1 S&P 600

Indeed, the worst crash since covid was all about President Trump’s “Liberation Day” tariff announcement landing heavier than expected. Making the 1930 Smoot-Hawley effort look like a bake sale, he raised America’s tariff wall by sevenfold, to an average import duty of nearly 23%, up from a quaint 3%.

According to the World Trade Organization, here’s the new landscape, before retaliatory fun begins:

Average Tariff Rates (%)

23 USA

12 India

8.4 South Korea

6.7 Brazil

6.3 Thailand

6.1 Saudi Arabia

6.0 Chile

5.3 Russia

5.1 Vietnam

3.9 Mexico

3.4 Canada

3.3 UK

3.0 China

2.7 EU

1.9 Japan

The VIX volatility gauge took one look at that and vaulted like a biotech stock with a rumor and no revenue, surging 40% from 21.5 to 30.

Wall Street’s dunk tank was set extra deep. The S&P took its worst drenching since June 2020 when second-wave covid fears spiked sanitizer sales. The Dow notched its fourth-biggest decline this decade. The Nasdaq fell its most since March 2020, when the Fed cut interest rates to near zero, confirming there was something to be nervous about.

Small-cap stocks officially entered bear-market territory, with the S&P 600 now down 21.9% from its November 25 peak, the very month when now-Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent penned his Wall Street Journal valentine to “Trump’s Economics.”

In it, he credited the post-election rally to “expectations of higher growth, lower volatility and inflation, and a revitalized economy for all Americans.” From last Sunday’s Kelly Letter: “Nothing crashes harder than high hopes…”

Retaliation threats are stacking up faster than disclaimers in a crypto prospectus.

China vowed a stiff response unless the tariffs are lifted. Canada, keeping it neighborly, will match the 25% auto tariffs tit-for-tat with an equal levy on American vehicles. The EU is calibrating a response. France’s prime minister called the tariffs “a catastrophe for the economic world.” From a country that’s handled downturns with decapitation, that’s saying something.

Mexico, however, twirled into a hat dance after being spared the rod, one of the few free-trade partners to dodge it. Avocado toast is safe, and really, isn’t that what counts?

The Trump administration remains serenely convinced that the duties' dragnet will yield lasting economic bounty. Commerce Secretary Lutnick assured CNN viewers that Trump will not back down from this “reordering of global trade.” The president himself told reporters, “The markets are going to boom.”

Bears replied, “Oh, there was a boom, all right.” They doubled down on stagflation warnings, brushing off the idea that recent declines have priced in the damage. No, they say, 2025 earnings estimates show valuations have plenty of altitude left to lose. Beatings will continue until ratios improve.

Fedspeak played along. Vice Chair Philip Jefferson detected uncertainty in the air and craves more data. Fed Governor Lisa Cook foresees lower growth and higher inflation, citing “tariffs and other policy changes.”

Let the trade negotiations begin, before the damage requires a stimulus package and a cold compress.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ENOR: Trending Well, With Favorable Prospects
data.description:
Norwegian Equities Have Gotten Off To A Good Start This Year, But ENOR Isn’t The Only Way To Tap This Terrain

The iShares MSCI Norway ETF (BATS:ENOR), which covers around 59 stocks (mainly mid-caps which account for 43% of the portfolio) from the second largest economy of the Nordics region has gotten off to a solid start in 2025; during a period, where global stocks haven’t really gone anywhere, and European stocks have done well to witness gains of 11% (on average), ENOR has surged ahead by 15%.

Note that ENOR isn’t the only ETF product around that gives you access to this hotspot of Norwegian stocks; you also have the Global X MSCI Norway ETF (NORW), which is more popular (AUM of $50m as opposed to $32m for ENOR), and has been around for a longer period (it got listed in November 2010, while ENOR followed suit only in January 2012).

Even though these products are backed by competing fund houses, note that they both track the same index - the MSCI Norway IMI 25/50 Index. In light of this scenario, it would make sense to go with a product that is structurally superior. Unfortunately, it is difficult to pick a clear winner, as both products shine in different ways. NORW is the cheaper of the two, with an expense ratio of 0.5%, but ENOR is more rewarding from a yield angle (a higher current yield of 5.48% as well as a superior historical track record). Ultimately, it will boil down to each investor’s preference. Are you someone who attaches importance to the cost economics, or someone who wants a higher yield?

What Do The Norwegian Macros Look Like?

After a fairly resolute Q2 and Q3, where Norwegian GDP had grown by 5.3% and 3.7% YoY, the performance in Q4 was disappointing, with growth declining by -0.3%, as against expectations of just flattish growth.

However, this will likely be a blip, as real GDP growth for the whole of FY25 will expand by 1.8% (better than the 1% growth seen across the whole of 2024). What’s particularly enticing to note is the healthy conditions of the Norwegian consumer. After benefitting from wage growth of 5%, last year, Norwegians will likely see another hike of 4% this year, which will help boost purchasing power.

What abets their position even further is the broad slowdown in consumer prices, which has come off in a big way from the +6% levels seen in 2023 (admittedly, the latest reading for February 2025 was something of an unexpected surprise, growing at 3.6%, the highest in 10 months, but one swallow doesn’t make a summer and all that). Basically, with the onset of positive real wage growth, don’t be surprised to see the performance of household consumption contribute in a big way (expectations of 2.8% growth this year, as opposed to just 1.2% last year) to Norway’s growth prospects. Already in January, household spending rose by 6.4%, the best performance in 9 months.

Meanwhile, the forward-looking consumer confidence metric, while still in negative terms, has been improving over time, and saw its lowest drop in 12 months.

So far, so good, but what could give these households a real fillip could be further cuts in the policy rate. We say this because Norwegian households are one of the most indebted households in Europe with a debt-to-income ratio of over 200%, and personal savings too is quite low at single-digit percentage.

The central bank was actually on course to cut rates in last month’s meeting, but the latest inflation reading undoubtedly put them off. Note that this doesn’t necessarily mean that the prospect of further easing has been taken off the table. Rather, it's fair to say that it has only been postponed, as the Norges bank is still budgeting for its policy rate to drop to levels of 4% by the end of this year (from current levels of 4.5%).

What Do The Valuations Look Like?

Despite the strong price performance this year, the valuation backdrop of Norwegian equities still shines bright, relative to their Nordic counterparts. Note that on a P/E basis, Norway is the cheapest option around, priced at less than 11x, a +20% discount to the peer set average.

Given this low multiple, one would also expect ENOR’s holdings to facilitate the lowest weighted average earnings growth within the Nordic bunch, but that certainly isn’t the case here. In fact, other than Danish equities (which are anyway priced at a premium P/E of 15x), none of the other Nordic regions are currently offering a better earnings runway than ENOR (long-term earnings of almost 10%, which is 200bps better than the peer set average).

Closing Thoughts - What Do The Charts Suggest?

Even the charts ENOR point to healthy conditions.

Firstly, take note of ENOR’s short-term daily chart. After spending the whole of February, just below the landmark 200DMA, it finally broke through and is now comfortably trading above it. This technical development would likely have stimulated a lot of bulls to come on board. This was then followed by another technical event - the occurrence of the golden cross pattern, where the 50DMA overtook the 200DMA. At this juncture, ENOR is in a good place for momentum players, as it is perched above all its 3 key moving averages.

Despite the rise this year, ENOR could still work as a suitable mean-reversion bet for European-focused investors, as its current relative strength [RS] ratio versus a broader basket of European stocks is still around 13% lower than its long-term mean of around 0.42x.

data.title: Semiconductors Winners And Losers At The Start Of Q2 2025
data.description:
The first quarter of 2025 was a tough one for the stock market in general for various reasons, but semiconductor stocks got hit harder than most as they look to make a fresh start in Q2 2025. While some may argue the selloff in semis presents a good buying opportunity going into Q2 2025 with many semis available at much lower levels, especially for those who missed out on the earlier rally, semis are unlikely to be in the clear just yet. Why will be covered next.

Semis underperform in an already difficult market

A previous article at the start of Q1 2025 mentioned how semis needed a lot to go right in 2025, which was unlikely to happen. Semis, for instance, are facing unique challenges that most other sectors do not. Semis have to contend with potential headwinds such as tariffs like other sectors, but they also have to deal with other challenges specific to the sector, like export restrictions to other countries and China in particular.

However, these limits are giving China an incentive to work on alternative sources of whatever is restricted, in effect giving rise to increased competition to many of the established players in the semiconductor industry. In the end, the article concluded the most likely outcome in the new year was for semis to underperform and end the year down.

A quarter has passed and it is fair to say that semis as a group have found it tough going in 2025. The chart above, for instance, shows how the iShares PHLX Semiconductor ETF (SOXX), which tracks 30 stocks active in the space, underperformed with a loss of 12.68% in Q1 2025. In comparison, the SPDR S&P500 ETF (SPY) and the Invesco QQQ trust (QQQ) also had to contend with a difficult quarter like semis, but they suffered comparatively less with losses of 4.55% and 8.28%, respectively.

The quarter was a bad one for semis, but it is worth mentioning how they got off to a good start in 2025. SOXX, for instance, was up as much as 10.6% for the year on January 22, but it is no coincidence semis have lost a lot of ground since then. The Trump Administration that took office was expected to pursue policies that are problematic for stocks, but they were also expected to be offset by other policies that should favor stocks.

It’s still early, but it's been more of the former, including an emphasis on the use of tariffs, and not as much of the latter. However, while not the only factor around, it was the shaking of the foundation underpinning the artificial intelligence or AI story that Q1 2025 will probably be best remembered by. SOXX, for instance, suffered its biggest drop in Q1 on January 27 with very heavy volume, which can be attributed to the release of a new AI reasoning model from China’s DeepSeek.

China was previously singled out as the most likely source of curveballs for the semiconductor industry and one was certainly thrown with the release of DeepSeek-R1 in January. Semis have greatly benefited from AI, particularly when it comes to certain names, but the entry of DeepSeek into the equation has put some strongly-held beliefs into question, as will be seen later.

Who was hot and who was not

Semis are down as a group, but some did manage to squeeze out gains as can be seen with SOXX. The 30 companies present in SOXX are Texas Instruments (TXN), Nvidia (NVDA), Broadcom (AVGO), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Qualcomm (QCOM), Intel (INTC), KLA Corp (KLAC), Lam Research (LRCX), Micron (MU), Monolithic Power Systems (MPWR), Applied Materials (AMAT), NXP Semiconductors (NXPI), Analog Devices (ADI), ASML (ASML), Microchip (MCHP), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company or TSMC (TSM), Marvell (MRVL), ON Semiconductor/Onsemi (ON), Entegris (ENTG), Teradyne (TER), Skyworks (SWKS), Lattice Semiconductor (LSCC), Qorvo (QRVO), Universal Display Corp (OLED), Onto Innovation (ONTO), STMicroelectronics (STM), MKS Instruments (MKSI), ASE Technology (ASX), United Microelectronics Corporation (UMC) and Arm Holdings (ARM). The table below shows the gains or losses at the end of Q1 2025.

INTC has struggled in recent years and it ended 2024 last among those present in SOXX with a loss of over 60%, but Q1 2025 turned out different with INTC coming out on top with a gain of 13.27%. INTC was able to achieve this because of how far it had fallen with a lot of negative sentiment, which flipped on its head after the optimism generated by the changing of the guard with new leadership, even if not much has fundamentally changed as of yet.

Speculation about a stock was also the reason behind the outperformance of runner-up UMC, which trailed only behind INTC with a gain of 10.17% in Q1 2025, except that the speculation revolved around a merger. Still, winners were the exceptions because only 6 out of the 30 stocks in SOXX ended with gains in Q1 2025. In contrast, 24 out of 30 head into Q2 2025 having suffered losses in the preceding Q1.

Of those who lost ground, MRVL was the worst off with a loss of 44.26%, followed by ON. The latter was adversely affected by problems, and a huge selloff in the stock, at an important customer, which happens to be the leader in the market for electrical vehicles in the U.S. However, the list of losers headed by MRVL is telling.

MRVL is one of those names whose bull thesis rests in large part on AI, together with names like NVDA, AVGO, TSM and others. All benefited greatly from their exposure to AI in past quarters, although MRVL has arguably not demonstrated as much benefit from AI as the other three, but this changed in Q1 2025 because all four underperformed the already underperforming SOXX. AVGO, NVDA and TSM still lead in terms of TTM gains, but the more recent struggles among AI plays clearly stands out in Q1 2025. DeepSeek left its mark in Q1 2025.

The table shows a few more things. Not only have semis lost the momentum they had until mid-2024, but they have gotten progressively weaker in the last three quarters. Most of the losses in Q1 2025 also came in the latter part, March in particular. SOXX, for instance, lost 9.76% in March and 12.68% in all of Q1 2025. On the other hand, the large losses suffered in the last month of Q1 2025 could enable a short-term bounce in Q2 2025, especially if semis can get some relief from the headwinds that caused problems in Q1 2025.

Are semis a buy in Q2 2025 after the decline in Q1 2025?

SOXX is close to 30% below the peak set in July 2024 and a number of semis, including some of the more highly sought-after semis, are down even more. Tariffs have weighed on stocks lately, but President Trump has shown flexibility on the issue. He delayed the initial tariffs on Mexico and Canada, for instance, and the stock market reacted well when he did.

Good news on the tariff front could trigger another relief rally, especially after the large drop many semis experienced last month with some like SOXX going almost vertical at one point. If the intent of the U.S. is for tariffs to be a temporary tool to extract concessions beneficial to the U.S. and the Trump Administration is seen to be working on other government policies that are more beneficial to stocks, like say tax cuts, then a rally is not out of the question for semis.

However, while a temporary bounce in semis cannot be ruled out, the March lows are unlikely to have been the bottom for semis as a whole. There is a reason why this could happen. The main goal behind the tariffs is supposedly to increase U.S. production of goods to lessen the need for imports. President Trump said as much when he remarked how companies can avoid the tariffs if they move production to the U.S.

Companies have the choice to move production, which cannot be done quickly, or pay the tariffs and decide whether to absorb the additional cost, or more likely pass it on to the consumer, but the end result either way will be an increase in prices for the end consumer. This will increase inflationary pressure, which is already an issue in the U.S. This could put the Federal Reserve in a dilemma.

The Fed has been easing monetary policy by cutting the Fed Funds Rate or FFR, which currently stands at 4.25-4.50%, down from a peak of 5.25-5.50% in July 2023, the last time the Fed hiked interest rates. The FFR was cut by 100 basis points in 2024 and, while there have yet to be any cuts in 2025, the most recent dot plots from March suggest two more rate cuts of 25 basis points each to bring the FFR to 3.75-4.00% by the end of 2025. In 2026, the FFR is seen to drop by a further 50 basis points to 3.25-3.50%.

In contrast, market expectations are even higher. Futures predict the Fed will be even more dovish by lowering the FFR by 75 basis points with an end point of 3.50-3.75% seen as having the highest probability in 2025. The issue here is that with all the recent government policies, especially those dealing with tariffs, the Fed may be in a lose-lose situation.

If the Fed cuts twice in 2025, let alone the three futures expect, the Fed risks pushing up inflation, in combination with the effect tariffs have on prices. If the Fed does not cut, the Fed can ease some of the inflationary pressure in the economy, but the economy will also slow down further due to the absence of the expected stimulus and the impact of tariffs on consumption. Both are undesirable options, but, given the Fed’s mandate when it comes to keeping prices stable, the Fed is most likely going with the latter. The Fed could be on hold for a while.

This suggests the market is headed for disappointment if or when the Fed decides not to cut three times as futures have priced in or twice as suggested by the dot plots. It can be argued that no single factor has contributed as much as the Fed has in enabling the bull market in stocks of the almost two decades since the financial crisis in 2008/2009. Conversely, if the Fed refrains from providing the stimulus expected, in this case interest rate cuts, stocks, particularly growth stocks like semis, are unlikely to handle it well.

Should semis expect even tougher trade restrictions from the U.S. government?

There is another reason why semis may not be in the clear. Tariffs have dominated the news cycle lately and they affect everyone in one way or the other, but there are other headwinds out there, including some that are specific to semis. Export or trade restrictions are another headwind for semis and there is reason to believe there will be more of them in the not so distant future.

First, it is important to mention that there is a strongly-held belief in the U.S. that whoever has the most compute will come out on top, whether it is in AI or some other strategic area out there. This belief is reflected in various actions taken within the U.S. For instance, U.S. export rules that seek to divide the world into haves and have nots by limiting the supply of AI chips to some countries, but not to others, have this belief as its core. So are things like Project Stargate, which seeks to invest $500B in new AI infrastructure in the U.S. There are many other examples.

If the U.S. has more compute, and everything related to compute like datacenter infrastructure and so on, than everyone else, then the U.S. has an advantage that proponents believe will ensure the U.S. stays on top. To ensure the U.S. has the edge in compute, it is necessary to limit what potential rivals can have, China in particular.

This means putting trade restrictions on anything that these potential competitors need to increase the amount of compute available for whatever that requires large amounts of it, like AI or whatever. This importance attached to compute is still present, including among decision makers, which suggests semis should expect more trade restrictions, especially whenever someone is seen to be gaining ground in compute, like in the case of China for example.

Is compute really as decisive as it is made out to be?

This race to have as much compute as possible has worked wonders for semis, particularly those engaged in high-performance computing. It is what caused, for example, a spike in demand for server GPUs from the likes of NVDA and CPUs from the likes of AMD, which were fabbed by a foundry like TSM and packaged by an OSAT like ASX using manufacturing equipment from the likes of ASML / AMAT / LRCX / KLAC and made from materials provided by the likes of ENTG, and so on. There is a spillover effect at work here.

It led to huge sums being invested in, for example, datacenters and everything else that goes in it, which in turn showed up in windfall sales at related suppliers. Some semis have expressed opposition to export rules limiting trade, but proponents of the policy have argued that companies will not be greatly affected because whatever is lost in terms of exports will be compensated elsewhere.

If, for instance, other countries do not have the compute necessary to service demand, then they will need to get it from somewhere with the means, most likely the U.S. So chip suppliers do not necessarily have to lose overall sales if they can no longer export because demand in the U.S. will be there to offset it. However, the hypothesis that whoever has the most compute is destined to win is debatable.

Compute is needed, but the amount needed to achieve a specific objective can vary greatly, depending on a whole range of factors. Someone may need more of it, but someone else could need much less of it to achieve comparable results. This can happen if, for instance, the latter uses a different algorithm than the former.

If A comes up with an improved algorithm with a time and space complexity of O(log(n)) and B continues to use an existing algorithm with a complexity of O(n^2) or O(n.n) to get the same results, then A will need less compute resources, negating the advantage B may have in terms of available compute. In other words, the argument that “whoever has the most compute will win”, which has been at the core of much of what has happened to the semiconductor industry in recent times, needs to be postfixed with “all else being equal”. If not, then it is not impossible to lose to someone else despite having the edge in compute.

Has DeepSeek changed anything for semis?

This is where DeepSeek enters the picture because it offers a reminder that it is not all about compute. While some may question the accuracy of some of DeepSeek’s claims, like in how much was spent on training, there can be no denying that the DeepSeek API is available at a fraction of the cost of what the other leading AI models go for.

This is consistent with DeepSeek requiring much less compute resources for training and inference because it relies on algorithms that are much more efficient in terms of resources needed than what other models use. If compute is important, but not all that is important, then that could affect how much resources, like capex, should be spent on it, and by extension the semiconductor chips needed for compute.

Semis have thus lost some of their luster due to DeepSeek. In a world where compute determines who wins, the importance of semiconductor chips cannot be stated enough. Paying very high multiples for selected semis can be justified since they are that important. But if compute may not necessarily determine the winner, semiconductor chips are still important, but not as much as before. Semis go down in priority accordingly.

It could also make investors think twice as to how high a multiple they ought to pay for a stock that may not be on the winning side in AI. The race to get to say AGI was until very recently thought to be between a few selected names, mostly the hyperscalers, but what the episode on DeepSeek suggests is that the breakthrough to get there might not come from the known players, but from someone most people may not even be aware of. There are no sure bets in AI since the race is still wide open, which argues against placing too high of a bet on anyone in particular because it comes at the risk of ending up with a losing bet.

If anyone wants to position themselves to possibly win the AI race, then doing so should involve more than just having the fastest chips and more of them than anyone else because this excessive focus on semiconductors, and the semis that make them, runs the risk of winding up on the losing side since other important areas get neglected. AI should not all be about compute, or semis for that matter. Not having the best chips is a disadvantage, but not something that cannot be overcome as long as there are other areas that can make up for it with good results, like analysis of algorithms.

Investor takeaways

The overall assessment at the start of 2025 was that semis would end the year down and nothing has happened with one quarter in the books to warrant a change in that assessment. Most semis posted losses in Q1 with SOXX losing almost 13%, but this comes on the heels of huge gains in prior years. There is room for semis to go lower.

The market still expects a helping hand from the Fed and the dot plots call for rate cuts this and next year, but this may not happen due to policy changes by the Trump Administration, which put the Fed in a tough spot in terms of monetary policy. If the Fed is forced to watch from the sidelines due to the risk of fueling inflation, and the economy slows down due to lower consumer spending as a result of higher prices, then growth stocks like semis could have further to drop.

The AI story underpinning the bull thesis for many of the most prominent semis is still there, but it suffered a blow from DeepSeek. The fact that many AI plays were among the worst performers in Q1 2025 says as much. The low cost of DeepSeek and other AI models out of China will make it even more challenging to generate returns on all the capital that has been invested. If the inflow of capex spending slows down because companies come to the conclusion there are little to no profits to be had, semis will lose a powerful tailwind.

The importance attached to compute has put semis in an enviable position, in both a good and in a not so good way. It has directed huge capital flows into the sector, but it has also caused governments to impose trade restrictions. This is not likely to change anytime soon. If anything, the U.S. government is likely to double down on the latter to slow down China’s progress in the field. China will respond by doubling down on ongoing efforts to come up with alternative options. DeepSeek is just part of that effort.

If Q1 2025 is to remembered in the history books, it could be in the sense it may have marked the beginning of end of the whole notion of compute as the ultimate decider of who wins in AI and other strategic areas of competition. There is a strong belief among a lot of people in the U.S. that having more compute than anyone else will be a decisive advantage, and a lot that has happened in the semiconductor industry can be traced back to it, but this faith in compute could be misplaced. Compute is important, but it may not actually prove to be the decisive difference.

Bottom line, if someone wants to put new money to work in 2025, then semis are probably not the best place for it. Individual names may do better, but the sector is more likely to underperform than outperform in 2025. Semis might make for a better investment opportunity down the road, but it is not likely to happen this year.

data.title: Simulations Plus, Inc. 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Simulations Plus, Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Tariffs, Tension, And Turbulence: Global Markets React To Trump's Trade Shock
data.description:
By Andrew J. Willms

President Donald Trump's sweeping tariff announcement has reverberated through global markets, prompting a rapid reassessment of growth expectations, inflation risks, and investment positioning.

The policy introduces a 10% universal tariff on all imported goods, along with country-specific tariffs of up to 49% on select imports from nations including China, Mexico, and several European countries.

Far from a tactical move, these tariffs appear to be a structural element of the administration’s economic strategy - intended to shift global supply chains, rebalance trade flows, and revive U.S. manufacturing.

But markets, which had been bracing for clarity, instead absorbed a shock. Trading partners have already signaled intentions to respond in kind, heightening the risk of retaliatory actions that could dampen global growth and further disrupt supply chains

Market Response

Equity markets responded swiftly and negatively. The S&P 500 fell nearly 5% on the day, while the Nasdaq lost 6%, underscoring the market’s sensitivity to sudden shifts in trade policy. Tech, industrials, and consumer discretionary names led the decline. International equities also slipped as investors moved to reduce exposure to escalating geopolitical risk.

Losses were most pronounced in technology and consumer discretionary sectors, though selling pressure was widespread. International equities fell as well, though to a lesser extent, aided by more modest valuations and reduced exposure to U.S.-centric trade frictions.

Investor Considerations

The scale and abruptness of these tariffs have raised concerns about their potential to disrupt global supply chains, increase consumer prices, and dampen economic growth.

That said, although today’s moves were significant, they do not yet constitute a breakdown of long-term trends. For example, the S&P 500’s 50-day average is still above its 200-day counterpart, although the gap is closing and may soon turn negative if stocks fall further in the days ahead.

Moreover, U.S. stocks are now down more than 8% year to date (based on the S&P 500 Index), but bonds have rallied. The Vanguard Total Bond Market ETF (BND) is up 3.6% so far this year.

Diversification across asset classes has been a tough sell in recent years as U.S. equities - particularly tech - outperformed dramatically. But 2025 is once again proving the value of not putting all one’s investment eggs in a single basket. It is a principle that is a key component of The Milwaukee Company’s philosophy.

Conclusion

The new U.S. tariff regime has upended market expectations and introduced meaningful new variables into the global investment equation. While the immediate market reaction was sharp, it was not irrational - markets are doing their job by repricing risk.

For long-term investors, the challenge now is not to predict policy, but to adapt to it with clarity and discipline. That means respecting volatility without fearing it, and seeking resilience over reaction. If history is any guide, the eventual recovery may favor those who stay invested with purpose.

Original Source: The Milwaukee Company

data.title: CrowdStrike: AI Set To Supercharge The Value Of Its Platform
data.description:
Why I love Cybersecurity

While artificial intelligence will do many wonderful things for humanity, it will also equip hackers and adversarial governments with new tools to carry out there nefarious objectives.

In the 1990s and 2000s, bots, i.e., viruses, were spread by hackers, and they were developed such that antivirus software would not detect them.

Once a viruses infected a computing system, it would either operate in the background of that system indefinitely, or it would be eventually detected by the antivirus software, which would address the breach of security and eliminate the threat to the system.

These viruses were capable of many acts, such as stealing logins and passwords, using the information resources of the system for DDOS attacks, and stealing other sensitive information like credit card numbers.

Today, viruses perform the same acts; however, they've been supercharged via the advent of AI technology, which allows them to adapt within the computing system in real time.

Instead of a "dumb bot," or virus, which relied on its owners' commands, or a simple code set, today, these viruses can actively think while operating in the background of their infected host.

Instead of relying on the owner for navigating a computing system to achieve a desired outcome, such as theft of sensitive information, they can now actively reason themselves, discovering new pathways to achieve their nefarious objectives, 24/7/365.

With more and more sensitive information entering the cloud, i.e., being stored in computing systems, the stakes have never been higher, and the abilities of viruses have never been greater, and this trend is set to continue in earnest for decades to come. That is, viruses, aided by the latest AI, are set to evolve immensely in the decades ahead, creating huge room for competitive differentiation among the cybersecurity vendors in the marketplace today.

First, AI experimentation is just starting to evolve into AI outcomes. Second, we're in the midst of a rapidly accelerating geopolitical AI arms race. And third, winning the AI war requires the very best data and a battle tested innovation engine.

Commenting on the shift from AI experimentation to AI outcomes, we're still in the early, but rapidly evolving innings of the AI revolution. Businesses and governments across the globe are looking for their AI investments to yield both improved efficiencies and novel outputs. At CrowdStrike, we're requiring every team and function to leverage the power of AI.

George Kurtz, CEO, CrowdStrike Q4 '24 (CY) Earnings Call (emphasis added).

With this as our platform, let's consider one of the preeminent cybersecurity vendors, CrowdStrike.

Understanding CrowdStrike

CrowdStrike Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:CRWD) is a vertically integrated vendor of cybersecurity products, such as its identity protection product, cloud protection product, and its SIEM product.

CrowdStrike's Platform Graphically Depicted

We can see, in the above graphic, how CrowdStrike has layered various products and capabilities onto a single platform.

That is, CrowdStrike has built a vertically integrated, multi-product platform, and this is important to note for a variety of reasons.

Let's discuss.

As customers adopt more and more products, which is easy to do within a single, unified platform, they integrate more and more of their business operations and workflows into the CrowdStrike platform. This creates an embedding/switching costs moat, such that they are very unlikely to churn.

We can see evidence of this idea in CrowdStrike's 97% gross retention rate, which is a measure of how many customers stick with the platform on a year over year basis.

CrowdStrike's Gross Retention Rate In The Bottom Row

As we can see, CrowdStrike's gross retention rate stands at 97%, despite the very public and damaging IT outage that the company caused in mid-2024.

A strong embedding moat, as measured by a 97% gross retention rate, suggests very durable cash flows, which pulls up on a company's valuation such that the "risk premium" required by investors, i.e., the earnings yield above the risk free rate, such as the 10 year treasury bond's yield, is lower.

Strong embedding moat = more durable cash flows = a bigger valuation investors are willing to pay, because they know they can count on those cash flows through thick and thin, i.e., through the ebbs and flows of economic cycles.

In addition to creating a strong embedding moat for CrowdStrike, its vertically integrated, multi-product platform, on which it offers currently 29 different products, provides a host of other benefits.

For instance:

  1. More products = more avenues to go to market = faster growth and more efficient marketing spend, as CrowdStrike can show up to the right customer with the right product, increasing its odds of winning the business.
  2. More products = bigger total addressable market. If CrowdStrike offered just one product, its TAM would be limited to, perhaps, $5B. But, because it offers 29, that TAM is substantially larger. I will provide data illustrating this in a moment.
  3. More products = more valuable customers, i.e., with one product, a customer might be worth $100M to CrowdStrike, but, with 29, that same customer might be worth $5B over time. This is important to note because CrowdStrike currently has 74,000 total customers, which grew 30% year over year. And many of these customers have only adopted a handful of CrowdStrike's 29 total products. I provided data for CrowdStrike's customer product adoption below. Even if CrowdStrike never added another customer, it could likely grow to $10B in sales and beyond simply by upselling its existing 74,000 customers on its current product set. The reality, though, is that CrowdStrike will very likely both grow its customer base and expand its product set that it sells to those customers.
  4. More products = more durable cash flows. If CrowdStrike only offered one product, especially in the world of technology, there would be a risk of that one product becoming obsolete, imperiling its cash flow generation ability. However, because CrowdStrike has a diversified stream of revenues from 29 different products, there's far, far less risk of its cash flows being harmed by one or a few products falling into obsolescence as the technology landscape evolves.

There are more benefits to being a vertically integrated multi-product platform; however, for the sake of brevity, I'll conclude here.

Let's now turn to a brief aside related to the CrowdStrike thesis, as well as all the next-gen software names I discuss with you.

Best Of Breed Tech To Benefit From DOGE

DOGE has, of course, been fashioned into a partisan issue; however, I would ask that you suspend your political predilections for a moment as I illustrate why the best of breed, next-gen software vendors are poised to benefit from the ongoing restructuring efforts of DOGE.

On CrowdStrike's last earnings call, as well as on the calls of a variety of software vendors, an analyst asked about DOGE's impact on their sales into the government market. This is what CrowdStrike's CEO, George Kurtz, had to say on the subject:

We find ourselves placed at the epicenter of a rapidly evolving demand environment. A new administration, a new wave of technology, and a new threat landscape necessitate all businesses to evolve their cybersecurity programs.

Consolidation, cost reduction and automation are now the accepted enterprise and federal priorities. These priorities are accelerating the shift from ineffective, narrow or duplicative point products.

George Kurtz, CEO, Crowdstrike Q4'24 (CY) Earnings Call (emphasis added).

I often share that software is a vast market that's not entirely constituted by the companies that are front and center in the public markets.

There's thousands and thousands of private company software vendors, who are often less efficient and less competent than their best of breed public market peers for reasons such as the public markets putting much more pressure on companies to perform to perfection, or at least as close as possible to perfection.

Pressure makes diamonds, so to speak, and the public markets can be something of a pressure cooker for companies: they must be excellent every. single. quarter, else their stock price, and by extension business narrative, gets thrashed.

As DOGE acts like a hurricane in the U.S. government, eliminating the ailing and inefficient aspects of the ecosystem, software vendors like CrowdStrike stand to benefit.

Let's now turn to a valuation exercise for CrowdStrike.

Valuation

Because CrowdStrike is one of the best executed software companies on earth, it's valuable to study its business to understand exactly what mechanisms have made it so great.

We've done this a lot already today; however, I'd like to work through these ideas via the four points of stock value, which will also serve to inform the assumptions we'll make for the valuation model below. Let's now walk through each of the four points of stock value together:

  1. Total amount of free cash flow per share, and margin understanding: CrowdStrike's free cash flow per share has consistently grown since it went public, and its margins have expanded as well. Following these four points, I'll share a graph of its free cash flow per share and margins, and I'll share the long term operating model the company shared with investors in recent years.
  2. The growth of free cash flow per share: Because CrowdStrike is a multi-product platform, who has demonstrated the ability to both organically develop new, growth-runway extending products and purchase growth-runway extending businesses, we can be sure that its growth runway remains extensive. Additionally, because cloud adoption remains in its early innings, and because malware is arguably the most dynamic and intractable issue facing businesses and governments around the world, we can be sure there's ample runway for growth ahead.
  3. The durability of free cash flow per share: As a mission-critical, vertically integrated, multi-product platform, CrowdStrike has a profound embedding moat, as evidenced by its 97% gross retention rate, which it's held despite causing one of the worst IT outages in history. This implies that CrowdStrike's cash flows are exceptionally durable.
  4. Our next best alternative's impact on CrowdStrike's valuation: The reality, notwithstanding the market's behavior today, is that interest rates are pretty low. The 2 year treasury bond, for instance, is below the long run American average interest rate of about 4%.

CrowdStrike's Long Run Target Operating Model (Note The Free Cash Flow Margin At The Very Bottom)

CrowdStrike Free Cash Flow Per Share & Free Cash Flow Margin

With all of this in mind, here are my assumptions for my valuation model:

Assumptions:

TTM Revenue [A]

Potential Free Cash Flow Margin [B]

36%

Total diluted shares outstanding [C]

256M

Free cash flow per share [ D = (A * B) / C ]

$5.91

Free cash flow per share growth rate

22.5%

Terminal growth rate

3%

Years of elevated growth

10

Total years to stimulate

100

Discount Rate (Our "Next Best Alternative")

9.8%

Below, we can see the results of the modeling using the above assumptions:

Discounted Cash Flow Model + Assumptions Input + Dilution Considerations

Notably, I used a very aggressive 22.5% annualized growth rate; however, I do believe this is achievable considering CrowdStrike's multi-product platform, massive install base to whom it can sell more and more products, and TAM into which it's selling its various products, which itself should continue to grow as AI makes defending against malware threats more and more difficult.

Additionally, I did not dilute CrowdStrike's share count any further.

With ~$4.5B in cash and just $750M in debt, as well as robust free cash flow margins and free cash flow generation, CrowdStrike has ample resources to reduce the impact of stock based compensation over time.

In fact, as it matures in the years ahead, it's likely that it begins a share buy back program, leading to a reduction in share count.

Lastly, I used 36% free cash flow margins, i.e., the mid-point of CrowdStrike's long term guidance for free cash flow margins, and there's some chance they come in closer to 40% over time.

With these caveats in mind, let's now turn to total projected returns.

Total Projected Returns

As we can see, I used a 35x exit multiple for the 10 year modeling period, and I used this because CrowdStrike will very likely still be growing in the mid-teens by 2033, and its free cash flow per share growth could be closer to 20%, as a result of the virtually inevitable buy back that will come at some point.

With all of this in mind, we're being offered about 16% returns today, which I find somewhat underwhelming, but perhaps I have just been spoiled by the gains of the last few years from CrowdStrike.

That said, I do like CrowdStrike today within the context of a diversified basket of stocks; however, I don't love it. Hopefully, we get more selling from the Nasdaq such that CrowdStrike falls below $300/share, at which point we'd be getting much, much more torque from buying the stock.

But, again, down from $455/share and after a year of share price stagnation, I do like it here.

Summary

Following the CrowdStrike IT outage of mid-2024, CrowdStrike's growth rate was called into question; however, its Q1 '25 (CY) guidance has largely laid those fears to rest.

As can be seen in the chart below, CrowdStrike guided to 20-22% YOY growth for 2025, with net new ARR (annual recurring revenue) growth acceleration expected in the second half of 2025, implying accelerating growth through 2026.

CrowdStrike's Guidance For 2025

As for risks, at this point, they go without saying:

Cybersecurity entails protecting businesses in arguably the most complicated way known to man today.

And the level of complexity and the stakes for those protected have never been higher, and they are set to increase substantially in the decade ahead.

So breaches, lapses in security, and IT outages of various kinds are likely to occur again in the future, and this is a real risk for CrowdStrike, as well as all next-gen cybersecurity vendors.

I'll also add that CrowdStrike's valuation still implies perfectly linear 20%+ growth; so, should we enter a recession, which is always plausible, tariffs or not, CrowdStrike's valuation could decline by 50%, and I would consider that normal.

All that said, CrowdStrike, as well as the cybersecurity industry broadly, is set to grow for many, many years to come, and its share price should likewise grow over time.
